column16: Consensus B+ is slightly generous. The concept is correct (slope+elevation masking for pavement zones + box filter smoothing), but _box_filter_2d (line 431) uses a Python double loop over every pixel — O(H*W) in Python, making it unusable on 1k+ grids. Codex A- is overgraded. The pavement mask formula is solid but the implementation has a critical performance bottleneck. B is more accurate.
column17: Line 431: smoothed = _box_filter_2d(heightmap, smoothing_radius). Lines 279-302 of _box_filter_2d: Python double for-loop over (h, w) computing integral-image lookups. This is the cumsum approach but with Python loops for the summation step, negating the O(1)-per-pixel benefit of integral images.
column18: Gaea Desert/Arid node
column19: Slope+elevation mask combo for pavement zones; returns mask for shader use; box filter in numpy (no scipy).
column20: Pure-numpy box filter via _box_filter_2d has O(H*W) Python loop — slow on 1k+ grids.
column21: Use np.cumsum-based vectorized integral-image computation.

column16: Consensus B+ is slightly generous. Three fold types (anticline/syncline/chevron) with random strike direction is a good feature set. However, folds are added linearly (line 776: result += wave * amplitude * sign) with no cumulative strain scaling, no plunge variation along strike, and no asymmetry. Real tectonic folds have amplitude that varies along the fold axis. Codex A- is too generous. B reflects 'correct concept, simplified execution'.
column17: Lines 757-778: For each fold: random angle, project coordinates onto perpendicular, compute wave (sin or triangular), add to result. wave * amplitude * sign is uniform across the entire grid — no attenuation along strike, no amplitude variation. All folds have identical amplitude regardless of orientation or interaction.
column18: Gaea Stratify + Tilt nodes combined
column19: Three fold types (anticline/syncline/chevron), random strike direction, chevron uses triangular wave.
column20: Adds folds linearly — real tectonic folds scale fold amplitude with cumulative strain and plunge along axis.
column21: Add plunging-fold support (amplitude varies along strike).

column16: Consensus B+ is slightly generous. The pool depression and concentric terrace rings are correct concepts, but the rings are perfectly circular (no asymmetry, no downhill bias), the terraces are uniform (same width, linearly decreasing height), and there is no water surface, no steam vent, no mineral coloring geometry. For comparison, Yellowstone-style travertine terraces have asymmetric, irregularly-edged basins. B better reflects the implementation quality.
column17: Lines 594-604: Concentric rings at ring_r = pool_radius + ring * pool_radius * 0.4 with terrace_mask from abs(dist - ring_r) / ring_width. step_h diminishes linearly: pool_depth * 0.15 * (1 - ring/(terrace_rings+1)). Perfect circles, uniform spacing, no noise perturbation on ring radius.
column18: Yellowstone travertine terraces reference (Hell Let Loose / Horizon)
column19: Pool depression + concentric terrace rings with diminishing step_h — readable geology pattern. Returns spring metadata for VFX wiring.
column20: Perfect circular rings; real travertine is asymmetric (downhill-biased, irregular ridges).
column21: Skew rings downhill; perturb ring_r by noise.

column17: Lines 508-546: Slope-weighted origin sampling, concave scar (squared falloff), convex fan deposit at 60% volume conservation. Downhill direction from gradient at origin. Scar is concentric circle (not elongated uphill), deposit placement doesn't trace actual descent path. The 60% volume conservation heuristic is geologically motivated. B is fair — correct concept, simplistic geometry.
column18: Gaea Slump / Skyrim landslide scars
column19: Slope-weighted origin sampling, concave scar + convex deposit fan with 60% volume conservation heuristic — geologically motivated.
column20: Scar mask is concentric circle (uphill vs downhill not asymmetric); deposit placement uses gradient at origin only — doesn't flow along actual descent path.
column21: Trace downslope path and distribute deposits along travel line; elongate scar uphill only.

column16: Consensus B is slightly generous. The Voronoi polygon ridge approach is physically motivated (frost heave creates polygon boundaries), but the implementation has significant performance issues: O(n_centers * H * W) Python-speed loop over all centers for distance computation (line 376). Uses legacy np.random.RandomState. No slope-aligned striping (real stone stripes align with slope gradient). The elevation mask is simplistic (linear ramp of top half). B- better reflects the implementation quality gap.
column17: Lines 374-377: for i in range(n_centers): d = np.sqrt((ys-cy[i])^2 + (xs-cx[i])^2); np.minimum(min_dist, d). This is O(N*H*W) which is quadratic in grid size for typical center counts. Line 365: np.random.RandomState(seed) — legacy API. Lines 386-388: elev_mask = clip(elev_norm * 2, 0, 1) — simple linear threshold, not terrain-feature aware.
column18: Gaea frost-heave micro-relief
column19: Voronoi cell boundaries as polygon ridges — correct physics intuition for permafrost pattern.
column20: Python loop over n_centers for distance (O(N*H*W)) — scales poorly; uses np.random.RandomState (legacy); no orientation (real stone stripes align with slope).
column21: Use KDTree or broadcast to single numpy op; add slope-aligned striping.

column16: Consensus B is slightly generous. The reef platform forms uniformly around the entire coast with no temperature, gradient, or depth gating — real reefs are biased toward warm, shallow-gradient shores. The _distance_from_mask function uses an iterative two-pass approximation (not true Euclidean), and the reef band formula is simplistic (1 - abs(shore_dist - width*0.5) / (width*0.5)). The early return for no coastline is good. B- better reflects 'works but with visible gaps'.
column17: Lines 648-662: shore_dist = _distance_from_mask(underwater). reef_mask = clip(1 - abs(shore_dist - reef_width*0.5) / (reef_width*0.5), 0, 1) * underwater. No temperature or gradient gating. _distance_from_mask (lines 305-329) uses Manhattan-approximated forward/backward pass, not true Euclidean.
column18: Horizon Forbidden West coral reef authoring
column19: Uses _distance_from_mask transform; reef band centered on half-width; clamps to sea_level. Honest early-return when coastline absent.
column20: Uniform reef around whole coast; real reefs are biased toward warm/shallow-gradient shores.
column21: Gate by tidal_range and distance-from-land to avoid reefs in deep-water-hugging coasts.

column16: Consensus B is slightly generous. 50 cavities over a full heightmap is extremely sparse for honeycomb weathering, which by definition is DENSE. Each cavity is an elliptical depression with squared falloff — correct shape but no interior texture or nested cavity structure. The slope-weighted placement is correct but the density is far too low for the named phenomenon. B- better reflects 'correct approach, wrong scale'.
column17: Lines 700-718: 50 cavities total, each placed at slope-weighted random position. Cavity is clip(1 - dist, 0, 1)^2 * cavity_scale * intensity. For a 256x256 heightmap (65K cells), 50 cavities covers < 0.1% of the surface. Honeycomb weathering is characterized by dense, interconnected pits — this is sparse random pitting.
column18: Honeycomb weathering decals (Megascans)
column19: Steep-slope weighted sampling, elliptical cavity shape; proper intensity gate.
column20: 50 cavities over full heightmap is sparse — honeycomb weathering is by definition dense; cavity depth flat (no interior texture).
column21: Scale cavity count with steep area; layer micro+macro cavities.

column17: Lines 456-479: Two-factor filter: mid-slope band (Gaussian at slope_norm=0.3 with sigma^2=0.02) * elevation contour boundaries (exp(-dist^2/0.001) per layer). Correct geology intuition — springs emerge where impermeable meets permeable at mid-slope. Layer boundaries are elevation-quantized rather than true stratigraphic dip, but the approach is reasonable for procedural terrain. B+ is fair.
column18: Hydrological seepage analysis (Houdini/Arcgis)
column19: Mid-slope band + elevation-contour boundaries — correct two-factor filter for spring emergence.
column20: Layer boundaries placed by elevation quantization — real geology uses stratigraphic dip, not level contours. Narrow bands (exp/0.001) can miss entirely at some resolutions.
column21: Use actual stratigraphy (from terrain_stratigraphy) rather than quantized elevation.

column17: Lines 120-224: Voronoi biome distribution with soft transitions, corruption fBm map with separate seed, building-plot flatten zones with padded radius (1.2x), per-biome climate params, name resolution via alias dict. The Voronoi is fixed-weight (no climate-driven rebalancing) and flatten zones are circular only, but the overall composition is solid. B+ is accurate.

column17: Lines 47-54: Checks BIOME_PALETTES first, then BIOME_ALIASES, raises ValueError with sorted known biomes. Clean alias resolution with proper error message.

column16: Consensus A- is slightly generous. The algorithm is solid (slope map + flood-fill clustering + gradient-derived rotation + proper height_scale), but the Python for-loop flood-fill is O(N) with high constant factor, making it unusable on production grids (512x512+). The cluster rotation uses only the center cell gradient, not PCA over the cluster. For AAA tooling (Houdini/Gaea), this would need vectorized connected-component labeling and oriented bounding boxes. B+ is more accurate — the algorithm is correct but implementation quality prevents production use.
column17: Lines 506-531: Pure Python flood-fill with stack.pop() per cell — O(steep_cells) but Python-speed. Lines 562-565: face_angle = atan2(dy[ri,ci], dx[ri,ci]) uses single-cell gradient at cluster center, not PCA-fitted orientation of the cluster. TERR-001 fix (line 536) is good — gradient precomputed outside loop.

column19: Real slope-map gradient + flood-fill clustering + gradient-derived facing angle. Includes dimensional-units bug fix (TERR-001) with proper height_scale propagation.

column21: Switch to scipy.ndimage.label for O(HW) label pass and fit oriented bounding box via PCA of cluster cells for rotation.

column16: Consensus C is slightly generous. This is a flat plane with trivial Gaussian height bumps and a linear blend weight across X. There is zero biome-specific geometry — no roots, no puddles, no terrain variation between forest and swamp. The blend is literally x_frac clamped to [0,1]. Codex B is significantly overgraded — this would fail any visual comparison to Witcher 3 or UE5 biome transitions. Closer to D+/C- than C.
column17: Lines 270-282: z = rng.gauss(0, 0.15) * (sin(x_frac*3*pi)*0.5 + 0.5) — tiny random bumps. blend = max(0, min(1, x_frac)) — perfectly linear, no noise displacement of the transition boundary. No biome-aware geometry at all.

Arch is perfectly semicircular — no irregular mouth, no stalactites, no debris apron, no rock framing. Topology is correct (quad faces connecting rings) but geometry is basic. Codex B- is slightly generous; Opus C and the consensus C are fair.

column16: Codex at B is too generous. The geometry is a single sin-curved grid with per-vertex Gaussian noise. There are no fracture planes, no strata layers, no overhangs, no talus, no ledge variation. AAA cliff faces (Megascans scans, Quixel rock kit) have multi-octave noise with directional strata, overhang geometry, and weathering detail. This is a basic displaced plane — C- is accurate, not B.
column17: Lines 82-91: base_curve = 0.3*sin(x_frac*pi), noise = rng.gauss(0, noise_amplitude) * (sin * sin + 0.5). Single noise pass, single concave curve. No strata, no overhangs, no directional weathering.

column16: Opus was right at C+, the consensus B is too generous. The placement is UNIFORM RANDOM within area bounds -- no terrain awareness whatsoever. God rays should cluster under canopy gaps, fireflies near water, ground_fog in valleys. The volume area math is arbitrary (cone uses (h*0.5)^2, box uses (h*4)^2 -- these have no physical basis). All placement Z is hardcoded to 0.0 (ground level) regardless of actual terrain height. This is a basic randomized scatter with biome-themed rules, not AAA atmospheric placement.
column17: Lines 231-234: px = rng.uniform(min_x, max_x), py = rng.uniform(min_y, max_y), pz = 0.0. No heightmap input, no terrain mask input, no feature affinity. Lines 219-224: volume area formulas are arbitrary multipliers of height.
column18: UE5 volumetric fog / Horizon Forbidden West localized fog sheets
column19: Per-biome rules, area-based count calculation using volume footprint per shape, deterministic.
column20: Uniform random placement — no clustering around terrain features (god_rays should emit from tree gaps, fireflies near water). No occlusion/collision awareness. Volume area math for cone uses (h*0.5)^2 — arbitrary approximation.
column21: Accept heightmap + masks (ridge, water, canopy) and bias placement by feature affinity per volume type.

column16: Opus was right at C. The 'sphere' is actually just the 12 vertices of an icosahedron with no subdivision -- this is a spiky 12-vertex polyhedron, not a sphere. The cone face generation has a potential off-by-one in the wrap-around logic (line 371: next_next = (next_i % segments) + 1 where next_i is already (i % segments) + 1, so this double-mods). The box is correct but trivial. These are placeholder-quality proxy meshes.
column17: Lines 337-355: 12-vertex 'icosphere-like' with no subdivision -- this is literally an icosahedron, visually nothing like a sphere. Lines 369-371: cone face wrap logic: next_i = (i % segments) + 1; next_next = (next_i % segments) + 1 -- when i=7 (last), next_i=8 which exceeds segments=8 indices, so next_next becomes ((8%8)+1)=1 which is correct, but the conditional on line 371 'if next_next <= segments else 1' is redundant and confusing.
column18: UE5 volumetric proxy mesh
column19: Three shape branches (box, 12-vertex icosphere-like, 8-segment cone). Deterministic.
column20: Icosphere is actually a dodecahedron arrangement (uses 12 verts but faces have errors — 'next_next if next_next <= segments else 1' in cone logic is suspicious and produces degenerate tris). Mesh resolution is too coarse for any close-up.
column21: Replace with proper icosphere subdivision (subdiv=1 gives 42 verts); fix cone face generation math.

column16: Codex A- is too generous. The cost model is: total + particle_count * 2 + distortion_count * 5, with threshold-based recommendations at 15/30/60/100. These thresholds have zero empirical basis -- no frame-time calibration, no resolution dependency, no fill-rate consideration. All particle volumes cost the same regardless of particle count per volume. All distortion volumes cost the same regardless of resolution. This is a rough heuristic at best.
column17: Lines 411-426: cost = total + particle_count * particle_cost + distortion_count * distortion_cost. Thresholds are magic numbers. No GPU profiling data referenced. Does not account for screen coverage, resolution, overdraw.

column19: Simple linear cost model + threshold-based recommendation. Easy to tune.
column20: Uses arbitrary thresholds (15/30/60/100) with no ms-budget grounding; assumes all particle/distortion volumes cost the same.
column21: Calibrate against actual GPU profile data from a reference scene.

column17: Lines 620-641: Recomputes wave energy with hardcoded wave_dir=0.0 (line 625 — should use state hints), applies max 3m drop scaled by energy and rock_hardness. Differential erosion (softer rock erodes more) is correct geology. But hardcoded direction and fixed max_drop are real limitations. Codex C is too harsh (rock_hardness integration is meaningful); Gemini B+ is slightly generous. B- is fair.
column18: Gaea Erosion applied at coast

column17: Lines 585-608: Three-factor model: (1) Gaussian band around sea_level (sigma=5m), (2) above-sea filter, (3) directional exposure via gradient dot-product with wave direction. Vectorized numpy throughout. The physics is correct — wave energy peaks at exposed headlands facing the dominant wave direction. Missing fetch-length modeling, but the core model is sound. A- is fair.
column18: Sea of Thieves wave-energy modeling / Houdini coastal exposure analysis
column19: Real physical model: shoreline band (gaussian around sea level), directional exposure from gradient dot-product with wave direction, clipped properly. Vectorized numpy.

column16: Consensus A- is generous. While the function orchestrates 4 styles with distinct profiles, feature kits, and material zones, the underlying geometry quality is limited. The noise is hash-based (not OpenSimplex/Perlin), the cliff Z-step is a hard discontinuity (line 204: 'if land_factor > 0.3'), and the mesh resolution is coarse (resolution_across = max(4, resolution//2)). The feature placement is random uniform, not terrain-aware. Compared to SpeedTree coastal kit or World Creator, this is solid B+ territory — functional multi-style system with basic geometry.
column17: Lines 203-209: Cliff elevation uses hard threshold: 'if land_factor > 0.3: z = base_elev * min(1, (land_factor-0.3)/0.1)' — this creates a visually obvious step. Line 96-98: _hash_noise uses sin()*43758.5453 — a common shader trick but low quality vs proper coherent noise. Lines 507-508: resolution_across = max(4, resolution // 2) — at resolution=64, that's only 32 vertices across.

column21: Swap hash_noise for proper 2D OpenSimplex; smooth cliff z-profile; parameterize per-style in a config dict.

column16: Correct XY-projected closest-point with cumulative t output and degenerate handling. But it is O(N) brute-force scan over every polyline segment per query -- AAA uses spatial acceleration (BVH/grid). XY-only projection is incorrect for vertical splines. Gemini's A ignores the performance issue that makes this impractical for dense meshes.
column17: Lines 204-242: Linear scan over every segment. No BVH, no uniform grid, no spatial partitioning. Correct math but O(N) per query, O(V*N) for all vertices.
column18: Houdini xyzdist() / UE Landscape Spline closest-point query
column19: Correct XY-projected segment closest-point with normalized t-along-spline output and proper degenerate-segment handling.
column20: Brute-force O(N*M) scan of every polyline segment per vertex — AAA toolchains use spatial acceleration (BVH or uniform grid) and typically query in 3D not just XY.
column21: Build a segment AABB grid or BVH once per spline and query per-vertex; optionally offer 3D distance for vertical splines.

column16: Clean Catmull-Rom-style Bezier evaluation but uses fixed uniform parameter sampling -- no arc-length reparameterization, so point density clumps on tight curves and spreads on straights. AAA spline tools sample by arc length. No adaptive subdivision for curvature. Gemini A is too generous.
column17: Lines 153-157: uniform t = i/samples_per_segment sampling with no arc-length correction. Functional but produces uneven point spacing which matters for road/river width consistency.
column18: Houdini Spline SOP / UE5 Spline Component evaluation
column19: Clean Catmull-Rom-style tangent estimation via cubic Bezier segments with configurable tension produces C1-smooth polylines suitable for road/river paths.
column20: Uses fixed uniform parameter sampling (no arc-length reparameterization), so point density clumps on tight curves and spreads on straights — AAA spline tooling samples by arc length for even corridor width.
column21: Add arc-length reparameterization pass (cumulative segment length lookup) and expose a tolerance-based adaptive subdivision for curvature.

column16: Proper ordered blend with ADD/SUBTRACT/MAX/MIN/MULTIPLY modes. But nearest-neighbor resize (lines 624-635) produces blocky aliasing. MULTIPLY is idiosyncratic (1+lh, line 648). No OVERLAY/SCREEN/SOFT-LIGHT modes. No per-layer mask channel. Gemini A overstates it.
column17: Nearest-neighbor resize at lines 624-635 using integer index mapping. MULTIPLY at line 648: result * (1.0 + lh) -- non-standard. Missing overlay/screen/soft-light blend modes. No mask support.
column18: Gaea Combine node / Houdini Heightfield Layer merge
column19: Proper ordered blend pipeline with ADD/SUBTRACT/MAX/MIN/MULTIPLY modes, strength scaling, and nearest-neighbor resize when layer shapes differ from base.
column20: Nearest-neighbor resize produces blocky aliasing on mismatched layers; MULTIPLY is hardcoded as (1+lh) which is idiosyncratic; no OVERLAY/SCREEN/SOFT-LIGHT modes; no per-layer mask channel.
column21: Use bilinear/lanczos resize, add overlay/screen/soft-light, honor a per-layer mask, and expose an explicit multiply semantic (direct multiply vs 1+offset).

column16: Sequential application means later zones fight earlier ones in overlap regions (last-writer-wins). No overlap resolution, no priority system, no spatial bucketing. O(Z*H*W) with Z zones. Trivially simple wrapper. Codex A- and Gemini A are grading a 10-line for loop too generously.
column17: Lines 1580-1590: Simple for loop calling flatten_terrain_zone sequentially. No overlap detection, no priority, no combined mask approach. 10 lines of code.
column18: Batch flatten across multiple building footprints
column19: Straightforward sequential application of flatten_terrain_zone with per-zone parameters and safe key defaults.
column20: Sequential application means later zones fight earlier ones in overlap regions (last-writer-wins) — AAA batch flatten resolves overlaps via max/min/priority or unified mask; no overlap warning; O(Z*H*W) with Z zones, no spatial bucketing.
column21: Build a combined mask with per-cell priority/blend and apply in one pass, or expose an overlap-resolution mode (max/min/weighted).

column16: Good vectorized numpy with smoothstep C1 blend, auto target height, and proper NaN/inf scrubbing. However, circular only (no ellipse/rectangle), isotropic blend, and seed parameter is unused dead code. Gemini A+ is excessive for a circular-only flatten. Consensus A- is slightly generous.
column17: Lines 1529-1561: numpy vectorized, smoothstep blend (3t^2-2t^3), source range preservation. But circular only (line 1532: single radius), seed unused (line 1506 docstring says 'reserved'). Good but not A-tier.
column18: UE Landscape Flatten brush / Gaea Terrace flat zone
column19: Vectorized numpy implementation with smoothstep C1 blend, auto target height from in-radius mean, and careful source-range preservation (no silent [0,1] clip) with NaN/inf scrubbing.
column20: Circular only (name says 'zone' but no ellipse or rectangle support); blend is isotropic and cannot match building footprint shapes; seed parameter is unused (documented 'reserved').
column21: Accept (radius_x, radius_y) or a polygon mask; add optional noise-perturbed blend edge using seed; support rectangular/OBB footprints aligned to building rotation.

column16: Thin wrapper around compute_erosion_brush which is itself C- quality. Inherits all the toy hydraulic/wind/thermal problems. No undo, blocks Blender main thread during slow Python erosion, doesn't rebuild normals after displacement. The handler adds nothing to fix the core weakness.
column17: Lines 912-981: Standard bmesh read/write wrapper. Calls compute_erosion_brush (graded C-) without adding any value. No normals update, no undo push, no async.
column18: UE Landscape Erosion sculpt brush
column19: Cleanly wires bmesh read/write around compute_erosion_brush with _detect_grid_dims-based reshape and parameter validation.
column20: Inherits all weaknesses of compute_erosion_brush (toy hydraulic/wind); no undo; blocks Blender main thread during slow pure-Python erosion; doesn't rebuild normals after displacement so shading looks wrong.
column21: Move compute into a numpy/numba path, call mesh.calc_normals_split or bmesh normal update post-mutation, and add a progress/cancel mechanism.

column16: Proper world/local matrix handling and axis-angle normal alignment. But single downward ray with no fallback (misses overhangs), normal alignment replaces entire rotation_euler destroying authored yaw (line 1471), no multi-sample averaging, no parallelization. Gemini A is too generous.
column17: Line 1471: obj.rotation_euler = rot_mat.to_euler() -- overwrites all three Euler angles including yaw. Single ray_cast at line 1448 with no fallback. No footprint averaging for noisy terrain.
column18: UE Foliage/Blueprint SnapToGround / Unity Terrain.SampleHeight
column19: Proper world↔local matrix handling for ray_cast, optional normal alignment using axis-angle rotation, per-object result reporting, and configurable offset.
column20: Single downward ray with no fallback (misses on overhangs/cliffs); normal alignment replaces the entire rotation_euler, destroying any authored yaw; no multi-sample averaging for stable placement on noisy terrain; no parallelization for large object lists.
column21: Preserve object yaw by only tilting to normal around the vertical; add multi-sample footprint averaging; offer a fallback raycast from object centroid and an up-ray option for ceiling snap.

column16: Wraps compute_spline_deformation with bmesh round-trip. But spline_points at line 413 are used as-is without matrix_world transform -- if the terrain is moved/scaled/rotated, the spline points are in the wrong space. No undo support. Gemini A- is inflated given the missing transform.
column17: Line 413: spline_points = [tuple(p[:3]) for p in spline_points_raw] -- no matrix_world.inverted() transform. Lines 420-436: standard bmesh boilerplate. No undo_push.
column18: UE Landscape Spline tool / Unity Terrain path carve
column19: Correctly wraps the pure-logic function with bmesh round-trip, validates object existence and point count, and clamps samples_per_segment.
column20: Operates on object-local coords but the pure-logic uses world-space spline points — no matrix_world transform, so splines break when the terrain is moved/scaled/rotated; no undo support or mesh-data integrity checks.
column21: Transform spline_points through obj.matrix_world.inverted() before calling compute_spline_deformation, and register with bpy.ops.ed.undo_push for editor undo.

column16: Handles arbitrary non-grid meshes by binning into a grid, which is good design. But grid binning loses sub-cell detail, delta lookup is nearest-neighbor (no bilinear), two dead lines at 1696-1700 (_ = max(..., 1e-6)), and radius_x/radius_y collapses to averaged flat_radius (line 1681) so ellipse support is fake. Gemini A is absurd for code with dead lines and fake ellipse params.
column17: Line 1681: flat_radius = (norm_rx + norm_ry) * 0.5 -- collapses ellipse to circle. Lines 1696, 1700: _ = max(..., 1e-6) -- dead code. Nearest-neighbor at line 1703: grid[row_idx, col_idx].
column18: UE Landscape Flatten tool on an arbitrary mesh
column19: Handles arbitrary non-grid meshes by binning vertices into a grid, fills NaN cells via column/global mean, and applies per-vertex delta to preserve mesh topology rather than reshape-assuming row-major.
column20: Grid binning loses sub-cell detail and reintroduces aliasing; fetches delta via grid[row_idx, col_idx] nearest-neighbor (no bilinear); target_height normalization recomputes z_min/z_max on the binned grid not the vertices; two dead `_ = max(..., 1e-6)` lines signal unfinished normalization logic; ellipse radius_x/radius_y collapses to a single averaged flat_radius so non-circular zones aren't actually supported despite the param surface.
column21: Use bilinear interpolation when sampling delta back to vertices, honor radius_x vs radius_y as a true ellipse in the pure-logic call, remove dead code, and compute target normalization from the real vertex Z range.

column16: Full CRUD routing is good, but every mutation re-serializes the entire layer stack to JSON on a custom property (line 711, 719, 749). This bloats .blend files and will fail at ID-property size limits. flatten_layers assumes vertex order equals row-major reshape. No undo integration. B is too generous.
column17: Lines 711, 719, 749: json.dumps([L.to_dict() for L in layers]) on every operation. flatten at line 761 uses reshape(rows, cols) assuming row-major vertex order. No undo.
column18: UE Landscape layer UI / Gaea layer panel
column19: Full CRUD routing (add/remove/modify/flatten/list) with JSON-serialized state persisted on the mesh custom property and layer-name lookup.
column20: Every mutation re-serializes the full layer stack to a JSON string on an ID-property — will bloat .blend files and fail silently at ID-property size limits; flatten_layers assumes vertex order equals row-major reshape which breaks on non-grid meshes despite _detect_grid_dims; no undo integration.
column21: Persist layers as an external .npz per terrain keyed by hash, chunk large layers, and add depsgraph-aware undo.

column16: Standard bmesh wrapper reusing compute_stamp_heightmap + apply_stamp_to_heightmap. Inherits the dead-falloff-parameter bug from apply_stamp_to_heightmap, hardcoded 64x64 resolution (line 1356), no rotation, uses obj.dimensions which breaks when mesh is transformed. Gemini A- is wrong.
column17: Line 1356: compute_stamp_heightmap(stamp_type, 64, ...) -- hardcoded 64. Inherits apply_stamp_to_heightmap's inert falloff bug. Lines 1366-1369: terrain_size=(dims.x, dims.y), terrain_origin=(obj.location.x, obj.location.y) -- no matrix_world.
column18: UE Landscape Stamp tool
column19: Clean bmesh integration, parameter defaults, and reuse of pure-logic compute_stamp_heightmap + apply_stamp_to_heightmap.
column20: Inherits the inert-falloff bug and primitive stamp shapes from its dependencies; hardcoded 64x64 stamp resolution; no rotation; uses object-local terrain_size from obj.dimensions which breaks when mesh is transformed.
column21: Plumb resolution/rotation/blend-mode params, transform position through inverse matrix_world, and fix the falloff degenerate blend.

column16: It creates a simple 8-vertex box mesh. Correctly documented as a marker/parent object, not visible geometry. But the mesh itself is trivial -- A- suggests production quality for what is fundamentally a bpy.data.meshes.new with 8 hardcoded verts. B+ is fairer for clean code that does a simple thing well.
column17: Lines 1094-1124: 8 hardcoded verts, 6 faces for a box. Clean bpy boilerplate with try/except for test environments. Documented as invisible marker mesh.
Constructs minimal viable TerrainPipelineState with flat heightmap + seeded noise, single cave candidate at center, proper scene_read wiring. Well-documented purpose. B+ fair.
Inverse of _world_to_cell with 0.5 cell-center offset. Correct.
Sources from scene_read.cave_candidates with region filter. Falls back to nothing if no scene_read (no auto-discovery from heightmap). B is fair for this simple filter function.
Vectorized numpy meshgrid with proper zone.permits('caves') check. Clean protected-zone implementation. B+ fair.
Delegates to BBox.to_cell_slice -- clean one-liner. A is fair.
Standard coordinate conversion with clamping. Correct and simple. A- is fair.

column16: Framing rocks are just world positions + radius -- no actual geometry, no shape variation, no per-archetype material hint, no mesh generation. It returns metadata (intent) but the actual visual result depends entirely on downstream consumers. Codex A- and Gemini A are grading the metadata design, not the visual output.
column17: Lines 522-533: framing_rocks are dicts with 'world_pos' and 'radius_m'. No vertex data, no mesh, no shape. Purely metadata. Vegetation_screen is a boolean. Occlusion_shelf is dimension numbers.
column18: Skyrim/ESO cave portal kit
column19: Per-archetype framing count (2 or 3), computes lintel position from entrance_height, vegetation_screen predicate, occlusion_shelf intent.
column20: Framing rocks are just world positions + radius — no actual shape variation, no per-archetype material hint.
column21: Specify rock asset kit per archetype + size variance + biased noise for position.

column16: Vectorized np.mgrid footprint per waypoint is good. But this is a 2D heightmap-lowering operation -- it can only lower the terrain surface, not create true 3D cave interiors. You cannot represent a cave ceiling or passage with this approach. For AAA caves you need voxels or mesh booleans. The fundamental limitation is more severe than B+ suggests.
column17: Lines 471-488: np.mgrid per waypoint, delta applied to 2D heightmap. Line 488: 'delta = np.where(footprint & (local_delta < delta), local_delta, delta)' -- only lowers surface. Cannot represent a cave roof or enclosed volume. Fundamental 2.5D limitation.
column18: Voxel carve along spline (UE5 Voxel Plugin)
column19: Proper vectorized np.mgrid footprint per waypoint; taper by distance from path center; deepest-delta-per-cell merge; populates cave_candidate bool mask.
column20: Flat 2D footprint — heightmap can't represent true 3D caves (the delta only lowers terrain surface). For real caves this needs voxel/mesh-boolean backend.
column21: Wire through to a voxel field (terrain_advanced) so caves are true 3D volumes.

column16: Per-archetype path shape is good design, but all paths are simple 2D polylines with trigonometric jitter -- no branching, no chamber nodes, no inclination variation within archetype. FISSURE just does uniform descent, KARST is vertical then horizontal, GLACIAL is sin() meander. Gemini A- overstates these simple parametric curves.
column17: Lines 373-428: Each archetype is a simple for loop with linear/sinusoidal interpolation. No branching, no chamber nodes, no Y-splits, no 3D complexity beyond gentle z-descent.
column18: Houdini curve-based cave authoring / UE5 Voxel Farm cave curve
column19: Per-archetype path shape (LAVA_TUBE straight, FISSURE vertical, KARST plunge-then-arm, GLACIAL meander, SEA_GROTTO shallow). Deterministic rng.
column20: All paths are 2D polylines with simple trigonometric jitter — no branching, no chamber nodes, no inclination variation within archetype.
column21: Add branching (Y-splits), chamber hubs along path, per-archetype branch probability.

column17: Vectorized np.mgrid per waypoint with max-merge. Preserves existing wet_rock. Linear falloff is simplistic (real seepage follows downslope) but functional. B+ is fair.
column18: UE5 wetness mask shader driver
column19: Vectorized np.mgrid per waypoint with proper max-merge; preserves existing wet_rock.
column20: Linear falloff — real damp patterns follow seepage paths (downslope preference).
column21: Bias falloff downslope of path.

column16: Uses cave_candidate channel to find positions, delegates to generate_cave_entrance_mesh. But sampling is random choice with no spatial distribution check -- could cluster entrances. Width/height/depth are random uniform with no archetype awareness. Between B and B+, B is fairer.
column17: Lines 934-950: rng.choice for entrance positions, rng.uniform for width/height/depth. No minimum spacing, no archetype-driven sizing.
MCP handler adapter bridging compose_map dispatch to pass_caves. Clean error handling, structured return payload. Uses synthetic state (not real scene), chamber mesh is a simple box. B is fair.

column19: Bridges params dict to pass_caves via synthetic state; returns debris/frame geometry intent.

column21: Prefer actual scene state over synthetic; richer chamber mesh.

column17: Clean factory function: loads archetype defaults, applies user overrides, returns typed dataclass. Well-designed API. A is fair.

End-to-end pipeline: entrance candidates, per-cave deterministic seed, protected zone checks, archetype selection, path+carve+frame+debris+damp, cave_candidate masking, side_effects, validation, PassResult with metrics. Well-structured pass. Only processes scene_read entrances (no auto-discovery) limits it. B+ is fair.
column18: UE PCG cave authoring graph
column19: End-to-end: scene_read-derived entrances, per-cave deterministic seed via hash-mix, protected-zone cell check, archetype pick, path+carve+frame+debris+damp, cave_candidate protected masking, side_effects recorded for downstream bundles, validation.
column20: Only processes scene-read supplied entrances (no auto-discovery from heightmap features); cell_count recomputed per loop.
column21: Add auto-entrance detection using cliff-edge + slope + basin proximity when scene_read is empty.

column17: Multi-factor scoring (altitude, slope, wetness, basin, concavity) with deterministic jitter tiebreak. Heuristics are reasonable but hand-tuned magic numbers. Between B and C+, B- feels right.
Clean pass registration with proper channel declarations, seed_namespace, and metadata. Nothing to criticize.

column16: Deterministic, density-scaled, capped at 200 points. But no clustering (real debris piles up), no collision with entrance frame, all debris are the same type (just position tuples). Gemini A is too generous for what is essentially a random scatter along a path.
column17: Lines 593-608: Random path segment + lateral jitter. No Poisson clustering, no size/type variation, no collision checks. Returns bare (x,y,z) tuples.
column18: UE5 PCG debris scatter
column19: Deterministic, density-scaled, normal-jitter around path; caps count 200 to bound work.
column20: No clustering (debris typically piles), no collision with entrance frame; all debris same type.
column21: Add Poisson clustering + per-cluster type.

column16: Clean validation with hard/soft issue classification. But only 4 checks (framing count, lip height, damp mask, occlusion shelf). No geometry validation, no overlap detection, no terrain-context checks (is the entrance facing a cliff? is it underwater?). A is slightly generous for 4 simple threshold checks.
column17: Lines 681-731: 4 checks -- framing count >= min, lip >= 1m, wet_rock populated, shelf depth > 0. No spatial validation, no context-aware checks.
Standard FBM with opensimplex, configurable octaves/persistence/lacunarity, proper normalization. Correct implementation. A- fair.
Opensimplex noise wrapper with seed-based caching via global state. Functional but the global mutable state (_features_gen, _features_seed) is not thread-safe. B+ fair.

column16: Canyon is a straight rectilinear trench along X with only mild noise on walls. No meander, no talus slopes, no stratified rock layers. Side caves are metadata-only dicts (lines 213-229) with zero actual mesh carved. Material banding is just height-based (lower quarter = wet_rock). Resolution of floor is size/2 which is extremely coarse. Codex B is too generous.
column17: Lines 122-144: straight floor along X axis. Lines 148-172: flat wall panels with noise displacement. Lines 213-229: side_caves are pure metadata dicts with no geometry. Resolution at line 107: res_along = max(4, int(length/2)) -- for a 50m canyon, only 25 segments.
column18: Houdini Heightfield carve + side tributary erosion (e.g. RDR2 Grand Canyon, Uncharted Lost Legacy canyons)
column19: Produces a coherent three-piece mesh (floor + two walls) with wall-height-based material banding (wet_rock low, canyon_wall mid) and floor path waypoints useful for nav.
column20: Canyon is a straight rectilinear trench along X with only mild y-offset wall noise; no meander, no talus slopes, no stratified horizontal rock layers, and 'side caves' are only metadata dicts with zero actual mesh carved into the walls.
column21: Build canyon centerline via meandering spline, carve walls from a base heightmap with multi-octave stratified erosion, generate real boolean cave voids at each side cave, and add scree/debris cones at wall bases.

column16: Two-octave noise on face is good. Overhang lean at top quartile with underside sheet is structurally sound. But cave entrances are metadata-only (lines 619-632, no holes punched), and the ledge is a flat two-vertex strip with no thickness or 3D walkable geometry (lines 648-658). The overhang underside is only 4 strips deep. Codex B is too generous for metadata caves and flat ribbon ledges.
column17: Lines 619-632: cave_entrances are dicts with position/width/height/depth -- no geometry. Lines 648-658: ledge = 2 vertices per waypoint, single quad strip. Overhang underside at lines 593-616: only 4 depth samples. No boolean cave cuts.
column18: Uncharted / Tomb Raider climbable cliff with ledge kit, or UE Landscape Layer Blend with overhang tool.
column19: Two-octave noise on face, explicit overhang lean at the top quartile with underside sheet, and a separate ledge-path ribbon usable for climbing.
column20: Base cliff is still a single planar heightfield tilted at the top (not true overhangs with undercut), cave entrances are metadata only with no holes punched in the face, and ledge ribbon is a flat two-vertex strip with no thickness or railings/handholds.
column21: Use a displaced volumetric slab (front + top + underside stitched) so overhang becomes a real shelf, boolean-carve cave entrances through the face, and extrude the ledge into a 3D walkable shelf with chamfered edge.

column16: Latitude/longitude tessellated spheres with noise are fundamentally noisy spheres, not fractured rock polyhedra. Chain links are open cylindrical shafts (4-vert rings, lines 1710-1726), not interlocking torus rings. No flat-top island surface, no crystal geometry (crystal_vein material only). Consensus B- is slightly generous for what are noisy spheres with cylindrical tubes.
column17: Lines 1601-1633: latitude-ring sphere with noise displacement. Lines 1710-1726: chain link = 2 rings of 4 verts connected by 4 quads -- an open cylinder, not a chain link shape.
column18: Avatar/Horizon floating islands or Zelda TOTK sky islands; Houdini Voronoi-fractured rock clusters.
column19: Each rock is a real latitude/longitude tessellated sphere with two-octave noise on both radius and z, proper top/mid/bottom cap handling and even a fallback for mismatched segment counts; chain link geometry is actual extruded 4-sided rings, not just metadata.
column20: Chain 'links' are open cylindrical shafts stacked vertically (not interlocked torus rings), rocks are fundamentally noisy spheres with no sharp fractured planes or crystalline protrusions, undersides look identical to tops (same material differentiation is purely tag-based), and there is no island flat top / grass zone typical of fantasy float rocks.
column21: Replace sphere base with Voronoi-fractured polyhedra giving flat facets, differentiate top via flattened cap + grass mask, make chain links actual torus primitives interlocking at 90-degree alternation, and add crystal vein mesh geometry rather than just material tag.

column16: Pool bottom is a flat fan disc (not concave despite comment), terraces are 3 rigid concentric rings of equal width (not scalloped basins), vent cone is just 3 rings (base/mid/tip). No water surface mesh, no steam vent positions, no pour-lips. Better than C because the 3-tier structure shows intent, but not B- quality.
column17: Lines 1159-1177: pool is fan triangles from center to edge ring -- flat disc. Lines 1232-1268: 3 concentric rings with fixed mineral_rim_width spacing. Lines 1181-1223: vent = base ring + mid ring + tip vertex.
column18: Yellowstone-style travertine terrace (RDR2 Cotorra Springs, Horizon hot springs) with layered sinter steps.
column19: Three-tier terraced ring structure with per-tier z offset and radial noise, plus a cone vent with base/mid/tip rings and sulfur-crust material, which is more structured than a flat decal.
column20: Pool is a fan-triangulated flat disc (no concavity toward center despite the comment), terraces are only 3 rigid concentric rings of equal width (not scalloped basins with pour-lips), and there is no water surface mesh, no steam vent placement, and the vent cone is just 3 rings.
column21: Model terraces as stacked basin polygons with irregular pour-lips carved per tier, add a real water surface inside each basin, generate steam/sulfur plume emitter points, and vary terrace counts by pool_radius.

column16: Material banding in stalactites uses 'kt' variable (line 1867-1872) which references the OUTER loop variable 'k' from the cone_rings loop, but inside the face-generation loop (line 1858) kt is NOT updated -- it retains the last value from the vertex generation loop. This is a variable scoping bug. Additionally: refraction_zones (lines 1925-1930) are randomly sampled positions disconnected from actual curvature classification. Ice wall is flat (no thickness). No stalagmites, no pool, no volumetric ice. Codex B ignores the kt bug.
column17: Line 1867: 'if kt < 0.3' -- but kt was set at line 1837 (k / max(cone_rings-1, 1)) in the VERTEX loop, and is NOT recalculated in the FACE loop starting at line 1858. kt retains its last value from the vertex loop (always ~1.0 for the last ring). Material banding is therefore broken.
column18: God of War Ragnarok ice caves / Horizon FW Cauldrons with volumetric ice, parallax refraction, and icicle clusters.
column19: Per-stalactite cone with variable length/radius, noise-displaced ring geometry and tip triangles; ice wall is a genuine 2D grid with FBM undulation and a curvature-based refraction material split.
column20: Stalactite material banding uses `kt` variable that is actually the OUTER loop counter `k` from a previous loop (variable captured incorrectly — bug: `kt` in the mat_indices block references the last stalactite's last ring k/cone_rings, not current face position), 'refraction zones' are randomly sampled metadata disconnected from the actual curvature classification applied to faces, ice wall is a flat displaced plane (no thickness), and there is no floor/stalagmite, no pool, no volumetric ice.
column21: Fix the kt-scoping bug so banding tracks current ring properly; extrude the ice wall into a volume with frosted interior; add matching stalagmites and frozen pool mesh; derive refraction_zones from the actual high-curvature face centers rather than random sampling.

column16: Sinusoidal centerline with tangent-perpendicular cross-section is well-structured. Five-band material zones (hot_lava/ember_glow/cooling_crust/solid_rock) with height profile is good design. But it is a single non-branching ribbon strip, crust edges are symmetric, lava surface is the same mesh as rock banks (no separate flowing surface), and heat zones are uniformly distributed with no flow-physics grounding. Codex B+ overstates it.
column17: Lines 2025-2079: single strip sweep along sinusoidal path. No branching. Lines 2059-2070: symmetric height profile. Lines 2106-2121: heat_zones uniformly spaced along t. No separate lava surface mesh.
column18: Horizon Burning Shores lava channels or Kilauea reference with pahoehoe crust folds and incandescent fissures.
column19: Sinusoidal centerline with noise-modulated perturbation, correct per-segment tangent/perpendicular frame for the cross-section sweep, five-band cross-section profile (raised solid rock, raised crust, depressed lava) with height variation, and heat_zones metadata parameterized by flow position.
column20: Flow is still a single-strip ribbon — no branching, no pillow/tube morphology, no solidification fingers; crust edges are symmetric with simple linear height profile, no a'a vs pahoehoe variation; lava surface is the same continuous grid as the rock banks with no lip or embankment discontinuity; heat zones are uniformly distributed along t with no flow-physics grounding.
column21: Replace ribbon with a 2D flow-accumulation field producing branching channels, model crust as a separate floating shell above a lower hot-lava surface (two meshes) with broken crust patches, and derive heat_zones from channel-width narrowings/drop points instead of uniform sampling.

column16: The arch is a swept elliptical tube (torus-arc) with per-ring noise -- the textbook 'stretched torus' approach. Real natural arches are eroded rock masses with flat tops, scalloped undersides, and asymmetric pillar bases. Pillars are tapered 4-vert boxes (lines 1054-1067). This is a bent pipe, not an eroded sandstone arch. Codex B is too generous. Between Opus D+ and consensus C+, I place it at C-.
column17: Lines 970-1016: semi-elliptical sweep with ring cross-section. Each ring is an ellipse with noise. Pillars at lines 1054-1067: 4-corner cross-section with slight taper. No flat top surface, no scalloped underside, no asymmetric erosion.
column18: Real natural arch (Delicate Arch / Mesa Arch) as sculpted erosion of sandstone block, or Houdini Voronoi-fracture + erosion.
column19: Sweep uses a correctly oriented Frenet-like frame (tangent in XZ, normal perpendicular) and ring cross-section allows noise displacement, giving a non-trivial closed tube.
column20: The arch is a swept elliptical tube (torus-arc) with single-sample noise per ring segment — that is the archetypal 'stretched torus' approach called out by the rubric; real natural arches are eroded rock masses with flat tops, scalloped undersides, and asymmetric pillar bases, none of which are modeled. Pillars are simple tapered 4-vert boxes.
column21: Start from a solid rock mass (blockout box), boolean-subtract an elliptical tunnel, then apply per-layer strata displacement and wind-erosion noise on the underside so the shape reads as eroded sandstone, not a bent pipe.

Proper Blender node group creation with Blender 4.0+ interface API + <4.0 fallback. Implements the height_blend formula in shader nodes (subtract, multiply_add for contrast scaling, add 0.5, clamp, multiply by mask). Reuses existing group by name. Well-structured.
Standard slope angle from normal Z component. Handles zero-length normals. Correct Z-up math.

Height-based texture blending with contrast scaling (1x-20x range). This is the correct AAA technique -- grass fills rock cracks, snow on peaks. The formula (height_diff * contrast + 0.5) * mask is standard and matches UE/Unity terrain shader implementations.

A true A-grade pass executor would snapshot mask_stack before execution and restore on failure (transactional semantics). This is noted in the prior review but not reflected in the A grade. The typed Optional[np.ndarray] fields enable static analysis. UNITY_EXPORT_CHANNELS manifest enables round-trip to Unity. This is genuinely A- pipeline architecture.

Lines 456-480: Carves shallow trench along outflow waypoints. Linear taper (1-norm), min-delta merge (keeps deepest carve). Width derived from pool radius. Python triple-nested loop (waypoints * dr * dc). Functional but basic — no meander, no varying cross-section. B- is fair.

column19: Pushes shallow trench along outflow waypoints with proper min-delta merging (keeps deepest).

Codex A- is generous (no directional scour); Gemini C+ is harsh (the parabolic profile is correct). B is fair.

The algorithm is genuinely well-designed: joint drainage+drop threshold, D8 descent, confidence blending of drainage and drop scores, and D8-neighbor deduplication to prevent stacking detections along a lip row. The _ensure_drainage fallback is thoughtful. The main weakness is Python nested loops, but the algorithm itself is A-tier. Splitting the difference: B+ accounts for correct algorithm with slow implementation.

column19: Real drainage+drop joint threshold, D8 descent, per-cell confidence blending drainage and drop scores, D8-neighbor dedup to collapse lip rows.

No foam along the plunge path itself, no turbulence-driven patterning, no splash zone at impact point. Foam in AAA (UE5 Water, Horizon) is concentrated at the impact zone with directional spray and turbulence texture. A simple radial fill is C+ at best.

column16: Consensus B- is slightly generous. This is a simple radial falloff centered on the pool — a perfect circle with linear decay. Real mist from waterfalls is wind-biased, turbulence-driven, and concentrated downstream. This is basically a circular gradient fill with no physical modeling whatsoever. Codex B+ is overgraded. C+ reflects 'basic with correct intent but no physical sophistication'.
column17: Lines 488-512: val = max(0, 1 - norm) where norm = dist / mist_radius. Perfect circular linear falloff. No wind direction, no turbulence, no downstream bias. Identical algorithm structure to foam_mask. Python double loop.

Opus A- is slightly generous given the heuristic plateau detection and no overhang geometry.

Good intent, but only validates the PROFILE params, not actual generated mesh. Opus A- is generous (no mesh verification); Codex C+ is harsh (the validation is meaningful). B is fair.
